### Required
* [x]  The default cache location is `rappdirs::user_cache_dir(appname = "gemmaR")` (as set by `gemma.R:::gemmaCache()`) but is documented in the vignette as being `tools::R_user_dir("gemmaR", which = "cache")`. Please reconcile.

> Vignette is fixed accordingly.

```r
rappdirs::user_cache_dir(appname = "gemmaR")
#> [1] "~/Library/Caches/gemmaR"
tools::R_user_dir("gemmaR", which = "cache")
#> [1] "/Users/Peter/Library/Caches/org.R-project.R/R/gemmaR"
```

* [x]  The value of `getOption("gemma.cache")` is not respected by the caching functionality. I.e. if a user sets this to a non-default location then it is silently ignored; see reprex below.

> Using non-default cache locations used to require reloading of the package as stated in documentation. This behaviour is now changed so that reloading the package is no longer necessary. Documentation is changed accordingly


* [x]  It appears data are only loaded from the cache if `memoised = TRUE` in the subsequent function calls (see reprex below). The documentation reads like `memoised = TRUE` is only required on the first use to cache the data and that any subsequent calls to load the same data will make use of the cache whether `memoised = FALSE` or `memoised = TRUE`. I.e. I expected the cache to be automatically used if the data were already downloaded. It's not clear to me if this is an issue with the implementation or the documentation.

> Documentation is fixed to clarify the intended functionality. In function `memoised` argument's description is changed to "Whether or not to save to cache for future calls with the same inputs and use the result saved in cache if a result is already saved. Doing `options(gemma.memoise = TRUE)` will ensure you are always using the cache. Use `forgetGemmaMemoised` to clear the cache." Vignette is similarly clarified


* [x]  The top-level files `examples.txt`, `registry.R`, and `registry_helpers.R` are non-standard files, so you will need to justify their inclusion in the git repo. Tagging @Bioconductor/core for their input.

> these files were moved to `inst/script` as suggested by @lshep

* [x]  Upon acceptance, please update installation instructions in the `README` and vignette (it is recommended to only refer to or emphasise the 'official' Bioconductor installation instructions, to avoid issues with people installing incompatible versions of software when using the 'GitHub' installation instructions).
> the order of installation instructions have been flipped to make the bioconductor version the prominent version.

* [x]  Please add a `BugReports` field to the `DESCRIPTION` (typically, this would list https://github.com/PavlidisLab/gemma.R/issues).
> added

* [x]  The `vignettes/se.rds` file should be elsewhere (see https://contributions.bioconductor.org/docs.html?q=vignette#vignettes-directory-and-intermediate-files). Better yet may be to avoid relying on this precomputed result and instead actually evaluate the `dat <- getDataset("GSE46416")` code as part of the vignette (it only takes a few seconds on my system, so I don't think this should cause the package build to exceed time limits). If you have to use precomputed results then please follow the advice in https://contributions.bioconductor.org/data.html, in particular I think your use case is covered by https://contributions.bioconductor.org/data.html?q=data#internal-data.

> saved file and dependency to it removed

* [x]  Please check that the documentation of all function return values (via the roxygen tag `@return`) statements are accurate and sufficient.
  
  * [x]  Several man pages document the return value as `Varies`, which is insufficient. Please describe the various outputs the function may return and why.
  * [x]  What's documented in `@return` is not always accurate. E.g., `getDatasetAnnotations("GSE2018")` returns a _data.table_  not an "array of annotations" ('array' has a specific meaning within R).
  
> documentations now include proper data types when describing what they return.

* [x]  Please document which genomes (and builds) are supported by gene-based functions, such as `getGeneGO()`. I think this is probably documented on the main **gemma** website, but the **gemma.R** package should clearly state this, too.
> The latest date our GO database has been updated was added to the documentation. In the future this will be replaced with a endpoint providing the most up to date version. `getGenomeVersions` function was added to return the current genome versions for the available species. This function currently only returns information for human, mouse and rat genome versions.

* [x]  Why does documentation of `memoised` in `getDataset()` say,  "[SummarizedExperiment] vignette .. the ExpressionSet vignette for more details"? These vignettes don't have anything to do with memoisation.
> this was a mistake and is now fixed

* [ x]  Please provide documentation that describes how the DE analyses are performed  (i.e. the results returned by `getDatasetDE()`). I couldn't find this information in the package or on https://pavlidislab.github.io/Gemma/.

Information about how the analysis is performed is added to the documentation of `getDatasetDE` both citing the relevant paper and providing the relevant excerpt

### Recommended
* [ ]  Is it possible to return (or convert) the DE results as/to a standard Bioconductor data structure such as a _MArrayLM_ (from **limma**),
  _DESeqDataSet_ (from **`DESeq2**),  or a _DGELRT_ (from **edgeR**)? My understanding is that the DE results are precomputed and downloaded from the **gemma** service, but if it happened that the code used to generate these relied on these standard Bioconductor tools then perhaps this is feasible? If such conversions were feasible, then **gemma.R** would benefit from the broader Bioconductor ecosystem, e.g.,
  
  * Using `limma::treat()` instead of _ad hoc_ filtering of DE results based on logFC cutoff (see http://bioinformatics.oxfordjournals.org/content/25/6/765 for why the latter is not statistically rigorous).
  * Using `limma::volcanoplot()` or **EnhancedVolcano** instead of re-implementing volcano plots in the vignette.

> it is probably better to implement this as a future update as it will either require a change in the API itself or will require compiling data from multiple endpoints to populate the object

* [x]  In vignette, use `assay(manic, "counts")` rather than `assays(manic)$counts`.
> changed

* [x]   Please take all reasonable steps to address the `NOTES` produced by running `BiocCheck::BiocCheck()` on the package. In particular, I would expect these 2 issues be addressed:
  
  * Avoid using `=` for assignment and use `<-` instead.
  * Consider adding runnable examples to man pages that document exported objects.

> these particular issues have been adressed. all examples are now runnable.

* [x]  What's the difference between `getDatasetDE()` and `getDatasetDEA()`? I couldn't tell from the function names or documentation, but they return very different results (compare `getDatasetDE("GSE2018")` to `getDatasetDEA("GSE2018")`) I _think_ the latter is some sort of summary of the former.
> documentation for these functions are now clarified.

* [ ]  How do the gene-based functions compare to existing ways of using Bioconductor to achieve the same functionality. e.g., Why should a user use `gemma.R::getGeneLocation()` and what pros/cons does this have over more general Bioconductor-based solutions such as https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/workflows/vignettes/annotation/inst/doc/Annotation_Resources.html

> these are convenience functions designed to ensure compatibility with Gemma's databases only present to facilitate use

* [x]  Good job with your unit tests. You might take a look at `covr::report()` to get an idea of where coverage might be improved.
